projects.title: ezPolicy
projects.summary: Enterprise GenAI document intelligence system — accuracy leaped from <20% to >90%

sections.title: The Business Problem
sections.content: At my company, 3,000+ policy documents across Legal, Compliance, HR, Operations, and Technology created a massive knowledge fragmentation problem. Employees spent hours manually searching PDFs. The accuracy rate for complex multi-policy queries was under 20%. Every wrong answer meant compliance risk or operational delay.

sections.title: Research & Discovery
sections.content: We evaluated three approaches: (1) Traditional keyword search — too shallow for legal documents where context matters. (2) Simple RAG — improved retrieval but failed on multi-hop queries requiring cross-referencing. (3) Agentic RAG — the winner. An Agent-based system could decompose complex queries, search multiple sources, and self-reflect on answer quality.

sections.title: Technical Architecture
sections.content: **Core Pipeline:** LangChain orchestration layer → Router Agent decomposes query → Sub-Agents search OpenSearch (vector) + Neo4j (graph) → Self-Reflection Loop validates completeness → Synthesis Agent produces final answer with citations. **Chunking:** Semantic chunking at 512 tokens with 128-token overlap, preserving section headers for context. **Guardrails:** Prompt Security Gate with regex patterns, semantic classifier, and PII redaction.

sections.title: Implementation Journey
sections.content: Managed full SDLC: requirements gathering with Legal/HR stakeholders → UI/UX workshops for the query interface → sprint-based development with bi-weekly demos → handover documentation and training. The key challenge was embedding domain expertise — legal teams needed to validate the system's understanding of complex regulatory language. We built a feedback loop where flagged answers were reviewed and used to fine-tune retrieval.

sections.title: Results & Impact
sections.content: **Accuracy:** <20% → >90% on complex multi-policy queries. **Response time:** Hours of manual search → <10 seconds. **Adoption:** 60% HR ticket deflection within 3 months. **Scale:** Supporting 3,000+ documents with sub-second retrieval. Recognized as a bank-wide GenAI success story and referenced in Vietnam Digital Awards 2025.

demo.blogContent:
## ezPolicy: Building an Enterprise GenAI Document Intelligence System

### The Challenge

Banks handle massive amounts of policy documentation — Legal, Compliance, HR, Operations, Technology — each with their own formats, terminology, and update cycles. When I joined my company, employees spent hours manually searching through 3,000+ PDFs to answer compliance questions. The accuracy for multi-policy queries was under 20%.

### Why Traditional Search Fails

Keyword search doesn't understand context. A query about "customer data retention for credit card applications" needs to combine information from:
- The Data Privacy Policy
- Credit Card Terms of Service
- Regulatory Compliance Guidelines

A simple keyword match returns too many irrelevant results. A basic RAG system improves relevance but struggles with multi-hop reasoning.

### The Agentic RAG Approach

We built a multi-agent system:
1. **Router Agent** — Analyzes the query and determines which document domains to search
2. **Sub-Agents** — Each searches a specific domain (Legal, Compliance, HR, etc.) 
3. **Reflection Loop** — Validates answer completeness, cross-references sources
4. **Synthesis Agent** — Compiles the final answer with citations

The key insight: Agents can self-reflect. If the answer lacks information, they go back and search more.

### Technical Implementation

- **Orchestration:** LangChain for agent coordination
- **Vector Search:** OpenSearch with semantic chunking (512 tokens, 128 overlap)
- **Graph Relations:** Neo4j for cross-document relationship mapping
- **Guardrails:** Prompt Security Gate with regex + semantic classification + PII redaction
- **API Layer:** FastAPI serving the agent pipeline

### Results

- **Accuracy:** <20% → >90%
- **Response Time:** Hours → <10 seconds
- **Adoption:** 60% HR ticket deflection
- **Recognition:** Vietnam Digital Awards 2025 reference case

### Key Lessons

1. **Domain expertise matters.** Legal teams needed to validate outputs repeatedly during training
2. **Feedback loops are essential.** Flagged answers should inform model improvements
3. **Guardrails aren't optional.** In regulated industries, every output needs traceable citations
4. **Start with one domain.** We launched with HR policies before expanding to Legal and Compliance

projects.title: AML Chatbot
projects.summary: Hallucination-free Legal assistant — 93.15% query relevance, 85 active users in 2 months

hero.problem: Legal division needed instant, accurate compliance answers

sections.title: The Business Problem
sections.content: my company's Legal division handles Anti-Money Laundering compliance — a domain where incorrect information carries severe regulatory consequences. Traditional legal research took hours. A standard LLM solution would risk hallucinations in a zero-tolerance-for-error environment.

sections.title: Technical Architecture
sections.content: **Strict RAG architecture** — no generative freedom, only retrieved facts. Grounding: every answer required explicit citation to source documents. Guardrails: tight regex patterns preventing the model from generating content outside retrieved context. Confidence threshold: answers below 85% confidence were flagged for human review instead of being displayed.

sections.title: Results
sections.content: 93.15% query relevance. 85 active users within 2 months. Zero hallucination incidents in production. Full technical rebuild from a legacy system with improved accuracy and maintainability.

projects.title: Droppii Migration
projects.summary: Azure to AWS e-commerce platform migration — secured $8K+ MRR

hero.problem: Scale infrastructure for growing e-commerce platform

sections.title: Why Migrate
sections.content: Droppii's e-commerce platform on Azure needed to scale while optimizing costs. Azure's pricing model for their workload created unpredictable monthly bills. AWS offered more granular cost controls, better reserved instance pricing, and a broader ecosystem of managed services for their use case.

sections.title: Architecture Design
sections.content: **Target Architecture:** AWS Landing Zone with multi-account structure (Management, Security, Log Archive, Workloads). Compute: EC2 with Auto Scaling Groups. Database: RDS Multi-AZ with read replicas. Storage: S3 + CloudFront CDN. Networking: Transit Gateway connecting VPCs. **BOM Calculation:** Detailed bill of materials covering compute, storage, data transfer, and reserved instances — this alone secured $8K+ MRR by giving the client confidence in predictable costs.

sections.title: Execution Plan
sections.content: **Phase 1 — Discovery:** 2 weeks mapping all Azure resources to AWS equivalents. **Phase 2 — IaC:** 3 weeks building Terraform modules for the Landing Zone. **Phase 3 — Data Migration:** 1 week using AWS DMS for database migration with zero-downtime CDC. **Phase 4 — Cutover:** 1 weekend with DNS switch and validation testing.

sections.title: Lessons Learned
sections.content: DNS propagation delays caused 30 minutes of partial availability — always lower TTL 24 hours before cutover. Connection pool exhaustion on the new RDS instance needed manual intervention. CloudFront cache warming should be scripted, not manual. Log costs from verbose application logging surprised us — enable log level filtering BEFORE migration.

projects.title: Prompt Security Gate
projects.summary: Middleware proxy filtering malicious LLM inputs — 100% security compliance

hero.problem: Enterprise LLMs exposed to prompt injection and data leakage

sections.title: The Business Problem
sections.content: When my company deployed LLMs internally, every chatbot became a potential attack surface. Prompt injection could bypass business logic. Sensitive data could leak through clever prompting. Banking regulations required demonstrable security controls — a simple API key wasn't enough.

sections.title: Technical Architecture
sections.content: **5-layer defense:** (1) Input Sanitization — strip control characters, normalize Unicode. (2) Pattern Matching — regex rules against known injection patterns and jailbreak attempts. (3) Semantic Analysis — classifier detecting malicious intent regardless of phrasing. (4) PII Detection — identify and redact sensitive data patterns. (5) Policy Decision — allow, block, or flag for human review based on risk score. **Deployment:** Centralized API on ECS Fargate, integrated directly into CAIP chatbot middleware.

sections.title: Results
sections.content: 100% security compliance for all GenAI endpoints. Zero successful injection attacks in production. ~15 blocked attempts per day. <5ms latency overhead added to each request. Full audit trail of every prompt and response.

projects.title: IT Young Talents Program
projects.summary: 4-step technical assessment funnel — 92% retention, 38% promotion rate

hero.problem: Traditional hiring produced high false-positive rates

sections.title: User Problem
sections.content: my company's IT hiring had a 30% false positive rate — one in three hires wasn't performing at the expected level. The existing process relied on resumes and generic interviews that couldn't assess real engineering capability. We needed a systematic way to identify true potential.

sections.title: Solution Design
sections.content: **4-Step Funnel:** Gate 1 — Systems Thinking (case study analysis). Gate 2 — Technical Communication (explain a concept to both technical and non-technical audiences). Gate 3 — Practical Debugging (sandbox environment with real bugs). Gate 4 — Architecture Conversation (growth mindset and potential assessment). Each gate had a clear rubric and pass/fail criteria. Mentorship was integrated into official KPIs for senior engineers.

sections.title: Results
sections.content: Retention: 65% → 92%. Promotion rate within 2 years: 15% → 38%. False positive rate: 30% → 8%. The framework was adopted as the standard for all technical hiring at my company.

demo.blogContent:
## Building a 4-Step Technical Assessment Funnel

### The Problem with Traditional Hiring

For years, my company relied on resume screening and generic interviews. The result? A 30% false positive rate — one in three hires underperformed. Resumes overstate qualifications. Interviews favor confident speakers over deep thinkers. We needed a better way to evaluate real engineering capability.

### Designing the Assessment Framework

After studying assessment methodologies from Google, Amazon, and Stripe, I designed a 4-gate funnel tailored to our engineering culture:

**Gate 1: Systems Thinking** — Present a complex business scenario (e.g., "Design a notification system for 10M users"). Evaluate their ability to break down the problem, identify trade-offs, and structure their thinking. No code required — pure analytical reasoning.

**Gate 2: Technical Communication** — Ask candidates to explain a technical concept (e.g., "How does HTTPS work?") to two audiences: a junior developer and a non-technical stakeholder. This tests their depth of understanding and empathy.

**Gate 3: Practical Debugging** — Provide a sandbox environment with intentionally buggy code. Watch how they approach debugging: Do they read error messages? Write tests? Use a debugger? This reveals their actual problem-solving habits.

**Gate 4: Architecture Conversation** — Open-ended discussion about past projects, technical decisions, and growth aspirations. The goal: assess growth mindset and cultural fit, not memorized trivia.

### Rubric Design

Each gate uses a clear 1-4 rubric:
- 1: Struggles with fundamentals
- 2: Can solve with guidance
- 3: Independent and competent
- 4: Teaches or extends the solution

This removes subjective bias and creates a consistent evaluation standard.

### Integrating Mentorship into KPIs

The key insight: Senior engineers must be incentivized to mentor. We integrated mentorship hours, technical interview participation, and knowledge-sharing sessions into official performance KPIs. This created a virtuous cycle — experienced engineers grew from teaching, juniors grew from learning.

### Results (After 2 Years)

- **Retention:** 65% → 92%
- **Promotion rate (within 2 years):** 15% → 38%
- **False positive rate:** 30% → 8%
- **Adoption:** Framework became the bank-wide standard for technical hiring

projects.title: Arxiv Assistant
projects.summary: R&D tool helping data scientists digest complex academic papers

hero.problem: Data scientists spent hours manually reading dense academic papers
hero.outcome: Saved dozens of manual reading hours weekly

sections.title: Research Question
sections.content: How can we help data scientists efficiently extract insights from complex academic papers that contain dense mathematical notation, charts, and domain-specific terminology? Traditional RAG struggles with math rendering, chart interpretation, and the hierarchical structure of academic writing.

sections.title: Methodology
sections.content: **PDF Parsing Pipeline:** Custom parser handling LaTeX math notation, figure/table extraction, and section hierarchy preservation. **Multi-modal RAG:** Combined text embeddings with figure caption embeddings for cross-modal retrieval. **Structured Output:** Responses formatted with sections mirroring the paper's structure (Abstract, Methods, Results, Discussion) so users could drill into specific areas.

sections.title: Findings
sections.content: Math notation parsing remains the hardest problem — LaTeX-to-readable-text conversion loses semantic meaning. Chart interpretation via vision models shows promise but isn't production-ready. The hierarchical paper structure mapping was the most impactful feature — users could ask 'what methodology did this paper use?' and get a targeted, section-specific answer.

projects.title: Matcha Junkie
projects.summary: A full-stack matcha latte e-commerce shop with authentication, CRUD product management, shopping cart, and order logging — built with Flask and deployed on GitHub Pages.

hero.problem: Small matcha shops need a simple, beautiful online presence with product management
hero.outcome: Fully functional e-commerce site with auth, product CRUD, cart, and order history

sections.title: User Problem
sections.content: Independent matcha tea shops struggle to establish an online presence. They need a simple, beautiful website where customers can browse products, add to cart, and place orders — without complex e-commerce platforms or high monthly fees. **Matcha Junkie** solves this with a lightweight Flask application that provides all essential e-commerce features in a clean, green-themed interface.

sections.title: Technical Architecture
sections.content: **Stack:** Flask (Python web framework) + SQLAlchemy (ORM) + Flask-Login (authentication) + Jinja2 (templates) + SQLite (database). **Auth Flow:** User registers → password hashed with Werkzeug → Flask-Login manages sessions → protected routes for cart/orders. **Data Flow:** Products stored in SQLite via SQLAlchemy models → CRUD operations via Flask routes → Jinja2 renders dynamic HTML → static assets served from /static. **Deployment:** GitHub Pages — static site generation approach with Flask-Frozen or manual build.

sections.title: Key Features
sections.content: **Authentication:** Register, login, logout with hashed passwords and session management. **Product Catalog:** Browse matcha products with images, descriptions, and prices. **Shopping Cart:** Add/remove items, update quantities, persistent across sessions. **Checkout:** Order placement with customer details. **Order History:** Logged-in users can view past orders with status. **Admin Features:** Product CRUD (create, read, update, delete) for shop owners.

sections.title: Why Flask?
sections.content: Flask was chosen over heavier frameworks for its simplicity and flexibility. Unlike Django (which includes many built-in features we would not use), Flask provides exactly what Matcha Junkie needs: routing, templating, and session management — no more, no less. SQLAlchemy gives us a clean ORM without the overhead of a full Django ORM setup. The result is a codebase under 500 lines that is easy to understand and extend.

sections.title: Lessons Learned
sections.content: **GitHub Pages limitations:** Flask is not natively supported on GitHub Pages (which serves static files only). The solution was to use a build step that renders all pages to static HTML, or deploy to a service that supports Python. **SQLite in production:** SQLite works great for development and low-traffic sites, but for real production use, PostgreSQL would be more appropriate. **Authentication:** Flask-Login is simple but requires careful CSRF protection — always use WTForms CSRF tokens in production forms.
